text: I'm here today to talk to you about tips and tricks for robotic hiatal hernia repair. My journey to robotic foregut surgery was really a two year process. In twenty seventeen I started doing general surgery cases, really limiting it to gall bladders and inguinal hernias. In twenty eighteen I started making the transition to robotic surgery for all my foregut cases And that's when I started seeking out mentorship and case observations, had proctoring, and I was able to bring all that expertise back to my own practice. And by twenty nineteen, it was rare for me to do a laparoscopic case. One of the biggest lessons I learned while making that transition to robotic foregut surgery is setting yourself up well from the beginning can alleviate a lot of the frustrations of the learning curve for the robotic platform, meaning knowing where your trocars are going to go and how you're going to dock the robot to avoid collisions. So my trocar placement is similar to my laparoscopic trocar placement. I'll have my camera in the left paramedian space and two robotic trocars in the left upper quadrant, one in the anterior axillary line and one in the midclavicular line. I then place a Nathanson retractor through an epigastric port and another eight millimeter trocar in my right upper quadrant. In addition, sometimes I'll use an assistant port in the right upper quadrant to just kind of keep the flow of the surgery going when I'm doing suture exchanges or placing the mesh. I'll then dock the robot in a way to avoid collisions. I have to make sure it's not gonna hit the Nathanson and the arms are widely spaced apart. Then I'm pretty much ready to start the procedure. I'm gonna place the patient in reverse Schoen-Delenburg position, and I'm gonna start with my vessel sealer, extend, a fenestrated bipolar, and my caudier.

The surgeon begins the hiatal hernia repair by dissecting the crura, reducing the hernia sac, and mobilizing the esophagus. The crura are then re-approximated, with optional mesh placement. For LES augmentation, the short gastric vessels are taken down, followed by the chosen augmentation procedure, such as partial fundoplication, Nissen, LINX, or TIF.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the pars flaccida of the gastrohepatic ligament to expose the right crura. The peritoneal covering is retracted, and the vessel sealer is used to score down to the muscle fibers, facilitating the dissection of the hernia sac from mediastinal structures. The anterior covering on the crura is left intact. The dissection continues over the anterior esophagus using an avascular plane, ensuring preservation of the anterior vagus nerve. The left crura is scored similarly, opening the peritoneum overlying the crura. The surgeon spreads in the avascular plane on the anterior and right side of the esophagus, dividing vessels as needed while preserving the vagus nerve. A posterior window is created by gently spreading the peritoneal covering at the confluence of the crura, allowing placement of a Penrose drain around the esophagus for retraction. The posterior dissection is performed with careful visualization of the tissue plane over the aorta, identifying the posterior vagus nerve before division. The esophagus is lifted and vessels are divided, achieving a high dissection and mobilization of the esophagus with intra-abdominal length. The crura is closed with a V-lock suture, ensuring appropriate tension and closure without impinging on the esophagus.
The surgeon places a Phasix ST mesh over the crural closure site in a parasophageal hernia repair, securing it with Vicryl sutures to provide additional support for the closure.

The surgeon retracts tissue adjacent to the crura and scores the peritoneal covering down to the crural muscle, opening up the cruse along its length while preserving the anterior covering. The hernia sac is held in the right hand, and dissection occurs outside of it using avascular planes. The camera is adjusted to a thirty-degree angle for better visualization, aiding in the reduction of the hernia sac. The posterior window is created around the distal esophagus, avoiding excess stomach in the Penrose retractor. The camera is driven high into the mediastinum to visualize the posterior plane between the esophagus and the aorta. Circumferential dissection around the esophagus is performed to achieve adequate intra-abdominal length. The cruse is closed with a V-lock suture, addressing wide spacing and tension by suturing from the right lateral position to re-approximate the cruse to the esophagus.
